title: Neural network decoder confidence as a learned proxy for the logical gap
abstract: To utilize quantum error-correcting codes, a decoder must infer the logical sector from the measured syndrome. Beyond producing a hard logical decision, some decoders provide soft information that estimates the reliability of that decision. For minimum-weight perfect matching (MWPM), a common confidence measure is the complementary, or logical, gap. Here we test whether the logit of a graph neural network (GNN) decoder can act as a learned proxy for the logical gap. Using a pretrained GNN for the rotated surface code under uniform circuit-level noise [Physical Review Research, 7(2):023181, 2025], we compare its soft output with the MWPM complementary gap on the same sampled syndromes. We find that post-selection based on the GNN logit yields a lower logical error rate than one based on the MWPM gap. Shot-by-shot, the signed GNN confidence distribution resembles the signed MWPM gap at low and intermediate values, but assigns higher confidence to many correctly decoded shots. While both scores approximate the posterior log-likelihood ratio, the GNN confidence magnitude is closer to its ideal value. These results show that a neural-network decoder trained only on syndromes and logical labels learns both gap-like discrimination and a quantitative confidence scale, enabling confidence-based post-selection when MWPM gap estimates are unavailable, costly, or poorly matched to the noise model.
summary: This paper investigates whether the confidence score generated by a Graph Neural Network (GNN) can replace the traditional 'logical gap' used for decoding measurements in quantum error correction. By comparing performance on the rotated surface code, the authors demonstrate that the GNN-derived soft information leads to lower logical error rates and better calibration than the standard Minimum Weight Perfect Matching approach. This suggests GNNs are a powerful tool for extracting reliable confidence metrics from syndrome data.
main_problem: To determine if a Graph Neural Network (GNN) decoder's raw output confidence score can serve as a reliable, learned proxy for the established 'logical gap' used in quantum error correction decoding.
main_result: Post-selection based on the GNN logit yields a lower logical error rate than using the conventional Minimum Weight Perfect Matching (MWPM) complementary gap across tested noise regimes. The GNN also shows better calibration towards ideal theoretical estimates.
method: The study compares two soft information measures—the GNN's output logit and the MWPM gap—by testing their efficacy in post-selecting measurements from a quantum error-correcting code.
model_or_system: The analysis is performed on the rotated surface code using syndrome measurements generated under uniform circuit-level noise. The comparison involves decoding performance across varying acceptance rates ($\kappa$) and physical error probabilities ($p$).
key_observables: Logical Error Rate (LER), signed confidence distribution, and calibration slope ($\alpha$) relative to the ideal posterior log-likelihood ratio.
important_parameters: Physical error rate ($p=0.005$), code distance ($d$), number of stabilizer rounds ($r$), and acceptance rate ($\kappa$).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that ranking measurements by confidence score (rather than absolute scale) determines the post-selection advantage, and that the GNN can learn sufficient soft information without explicit knowledge of matching probabilities.
figures_summary: Figures compare LER vs. acceptance rate for both decoders; other figures show probability distributions and empirical calibrations of the signed confidence scores against theoretical expectations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem by comparing GNN decoding to MWPM gap estimation, presents quantitative results showing superior performance (lower LER) for the GNN, analyzes the shape and calibration of the confidence scores, and concludes with implications for general decoder design.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete example of applying machine learning techniques (GNNs) to extract physically meaningful soft information in quantum error correction, offering a scalable alternative when computationally intensive methods like MWPM are impractical.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Post-selection logical error rate using GNN and MWPM confidence scores. Results for the Z-memory rotated surface code for (d, r) = (5, 5), (7, 7), (9, 9), where r is the number of stabilizer rounds. Each data point is obtained from 108 shots, which limits the available acceptance rates for different distances. (a) Post-selected logical error rate for different acceptance rates κ for physical error p = 0.005. Shots are ranked by the MWPM gap and the GNN logit. The inset shows the relative advantage obtained by ranking shots with the GNN confidence. (b) Logical error rate as a function of the physical error rate p, at fixed acceptance rate of κ = 0.9.
FIG. 2: Probability distribution of signed confidence scores. Signed confidence distribution for (d, r) = (9, 9), at physical error rate p = 0.005 using 108

FIG. 3: Empirical calibration of the confidence scores. (a) Conditional logical-failure probability as a function of the confidence variable g, for (d, r) = (9, 9), at physical error rate p = 0.005. Curves show fits to Eq. (3). (b) Pooled calibration fit over all simulated configurations listed in Table I.
FIG. 4: Probability distributions of the signed confidence scores, same as Fig. 2 but for (a) (d, r) = (5, 5) and (b) (d, r) = (7, 7) at physical error rate p = 0.005.
FIG. 5: Conditional logical-failure probability as a function of the confidence variable g, same as Fig. 3(a) but for (a) (d, r) = (5, 5) and (b) (d, r) = (7, 7) at physical error rate p = 0.005.
